fact.label: Today's Cat Fact
items: Cats sleep between 13 and 16 hours every day.
A group of cats is called a clowder.
Cats have a third eyelid called a haw.
A cat's purr can help heal bones and reduce pain.
Cats can rotate their ears 180 degrees.
Kittens have 26 baby teeth; adult cats have 30.

items.question: Is Catinder free to join?
items.answer: Yes — joining the community and reading all educational content is completely free.

items.question: Do I need a purebred cat to join?
items.answer: Not at all. Catinder welcomes every cat parent, whether your cat is a pedigree or a beloved rescue.

about.headline: Building Trust, One Cat at a Time.
about.subheadline: We started Catinder because we believed every cat parent deserves a platform as thoughtful, safe, and caring as they are.
about.missionBody: To help every cat parent build healthier, happier generations of cats through trusted matching, AI technology, expert knowledge, and community.
about.visionBody: To become the trusted operating system for every cat parent in Southeast Asia — where health, happiness, and heritage meet.

promiseItems.title: Always Safe
promiseItems.desc: Every feature is built with safety and transparency as the foundation.

promiseItems.title: Always Kind
promiseItems.desc: We hold our community to the highest standard of respect and care.

promiseItems.desc: No hidden fees, no surprises. We are transparent about what is free and what is premium.

promiseItems.title: Always Growing

faqPage.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faqPage.subtitle: Find answers to the most common questions about Catinder.

items.question: Is Catinder free to join?
items.answer: Yes — joining the community, reading all educational content, and using daily interactive features is completely free. We are building premium features for the future, but free access will always exist.

items.question: Do I need a purebred cat to join?
items.answer: Not at all. Catinder welcomes every cat parent, whether your cat is a pedigree, a rescued street cat, or anything in between. Every cat is loved here.

items.question: When will AI matching be available?
items.answer: AI matching is our next major phase. Right now we are focused on building trust, knowledge, and community first — the foundation that makes matching meaningful and safe.

items.question: How is my data kept safe?
items.answer: We never share your personal information without your consent. All data is encrypted, and every form is validated and protected. We follow industry-standard security practices.

items.question: What is the Knowledge Center?
items.answer: Our Knowledge Center is a library of expert-reviewed articles covering cat health, nutrition, breeding, behavior, vaccinations, and the heat cycle — everything a cat parent needs.

items.question: What are daily interactive features?
items.answer: Every day we offer a Quote of the Day, Cat Fact, Daily Mission checklist, Cat Mood tracker, Community Poll, and Lucky Cat Card — all designed to keep you connected and smiling.

items.question: What will Premium include?
items.answer: Future Premium features include AI health analysis, heat cycle prediction, vaccination reminders, exclusive articles, verified breeder access, marketplace, and a digital pet passport.

items.question: Can I recommend Catinder to my vet?
items.answer: Absolutely. Our content is designed to complement — never replace — professional veterinary advice. We always recommend consulting your vet for health decisions.

vaccination-basics.title: A Gentle Guide to Kitten Vaccinations
vaccination-basics.excerpt: What to expect, when to schedule, and how to keep your kitten comfortable.

heat-cycle-101.title: Understanding Your Cat's Heat Cycle
heat-cycle-101.excerpt: Signs to watch for and how to support your cat with patience and care.

nutrition-fundamentals.title: Nutrition Fundamentals for Every Life Stage
nutrition-fundamentals.excerpt: Building healthy habits from kittenhood through their golden years.

cat-behavior-kneading.title: Why Does My Cat Knead? The Science of Comfort
cat-behavior-kneading.excerpt: Kneading is more than a habit — it is a sign of deep trust and contentment.

breeding-readiness.title: Is Your Cat Ready to Breed? A Complete Checklist
breeding-readiness.excerpt: Health, timing, and emotional readiness — everything to consider before breeding.

annual-health-checkup.title: The Annual Health Checkup: What to Expect
annual-health-checkup.excerpt: A calm, complete guide to what your vet checks and why it matters every year.

raw-food-diet.title: Raw Food Diet for Cats: Benefits and Risks
raw-food-diet.excerpt: A balanced look at raw feeding — what the science says and how to do it safely.

vaccination-schedule.title: The Complete Vaccination Schedule (0–12 Months)
vaccination-schedule.excerpt: Month-by-month guide to keeping your kitten protected during the critical first year.

heat-cycle-care.title: Caring for Your Cat During Her Heat Cycle
heat-cycle-care.excerpt: Practical, compassionate tips to keep your cat comfortable and stress-free.

cat-stress-signs.title: 10 Signs Your Cat Is Stressed (And What to Do)
cat-stress-signs.excerpt: Learning to read your cat's body language could change everything.

breeding-genetics.title: Understanding Basic Cat Genetics for Breeders
breeding-genetics.excerpt: Coat colors, patterns, and what hereditary health means for responsible breeding.

senior-cat-health.title: Senior Cat Health: What Changes After 10 Years
senior-cat-health.excerpt: How to adjust care, diet, and vet visits as your cat enters their golden years.

paragraphs: Your kitten's first year is the most important for building lifelong immunity. Vaccinations during this window protect against diseases that are entirely preventable — and the process is gentler than most new cat parents expect.

The core vaccines recommended for all kittens include protection against Feline Panleukopenia (FPV), Feline Herpesvirus (FHV-1), and Feline Calicivirus (FCV). These are typically given in a series starting at 6 to 8 weeks of age, with boosters every 3–4 weeks until 16 weeks old.

Your vet may also recommend non-core vaccines based on your cat's lifestyle. Indoor cats have different risk profiles than cats with outdoor access. Rabies vaccination requirements also vary by location and local law.

Preparing your kitten for the vet visit is half the battle. Practice gentle handling at home, make the carrier a comfortable familiar space, and bring a favourite toy or blanket. Most kittens handle vaccinations well when their owner stays calm and reassuring.

After vaccination, mild side effects like temporary drowsiness or reduced appetite are normal and usually resolve within 24 hours. Contact your vet if you notice swelling at the injection site, difficulty breathing, or symptoms lasting more than 48 hours.

Good nutrition is the single most controllable factor in your cat's long-term health. Unlike genetics or environment, what you feed your cat every day is entirely within your hands — and that is both a responsibility and an opportunity.

Cats are obligate carnivores, meaning they require animal-based protein to survive and thrive. Unlike dogs or humans, cats cannot synthesise certain amino acids like taurine on their own — they must get them from their food.

Life stage matters enormously. Kitten food is formulated with higher protein and calorie density to support rapid growth. Adult formulas balance maintenance needs. Senior formulas often address joint health, kidney function, and lower caloric requirements.

Whether you choose wet food, dry food, or a combination depends on your cat, your vet's guidance, and your lifestyle. Wet food supports hydration — important since cats have a naturally low thirst drive. Dry food offers convenience and potential dental benefits.

When in doubt, look for food that meets AAFCO or equivalent nutritional standards, appropriate for your cat's life stage. Reading ingredient labels, understanding protein sources, and watching for filler ingredients are skills worth developing as a cat parent.

Every cat parent knows the feeling: something changes, and you are not quite sure what it means. Building confidence starts with knowledge — and that is exactly what this guide is designed to give you.

The most important thing to understand is that cats communicate differently from us. What looks like a small behaviour shift can carry significant meaning, and learning to read these signals is one of the most valuable skills a cat parent can develop.

Veterinary experts recommend a proactive, not just reactive, approach to cat care. This means regular check-ins, consistent routines, and staying informed about what is normal for your specific cat's breed, age, and personality.

Community wisdom matters too. Many of the best insights come from cat parents who have lived through similar situations. Catinder's community is here to share real experiences, ask questions, and support one another without judgment.
Remember: you do not need to have all the answers. You just need to know where to find them — and to trust that asking questions is always the right choice.
